vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication, network services, or boot configuration and is unlikely to cause lockout or system instability.

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 022

# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi

    LOGNAME=$USER

fi

if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask  027

01-umask.sh
01-umask.sh.bak
colorgrep.csh
colorgrep.sh
colorls.csh
colorls.sh
colorxzgrep.csh
colorxzgrep.sh
colorzgrep.csh
colorzgrep.sh
csh.local
debuginfod.csh
debuginfod.sh
gawk.csh
gawk.sh
lang.csh
lang.sh
less.csh
less.sh
sh.local
tmout.sh
which2.csh
which2.sh

llm_verdict.message: The default umask is now correctly set to **027** in `/etc/profile`, and the existing `/etc/profile.d/01-umask.sh` already contains the correct umask value. This satisfies the OpenSCAP rule **accounts_umask_etc_profile**.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly adds or updates umask lines in /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d scripts, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. However, the implementation can be refined to avoid duplicate entries and to handle edge‑cases more safely.
concerns: Using a simple regex may unintentionally modify commented lines that contain the word "umask" elsewhere in the file.
The plan unconditionally appends a umask line to every script that lacks one, which could create multiple umask settings in the same file hierarchy.
The script does not verify that the target files are writable and may fail on read‑only files, causing the remediation to stop midway.
suggested_improvements: Use a more precise pattern such as '^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{3}' and ensure the line is not preceded by a '#'.
Before appending, check whether any umask line already exists in any of the sourced files to avoid duplicate settings.
Add error handling to skip files without write permission and log the missing updates.
Consider setting the umask value in a single central file (e.g., /etc/profile.d/99-umask.sh) instead of modifying every script.
